clean_story:
Simplest explanation wins. Unnecessary complexity loses.

The name Occam’s razor comes from William of Ockham, a 14th-century monk who argued that when faced with competing theories, the one with the fewest assumptions is usually right—like choosing a straight path over a winding one to reach the same destination.

Here’s how it works: If you hear hoofbeats, assume horses, not zebras or unicorns. Doctors use this logic daily. A cough is more likely a cold than a rare disease. Engineers apply it too—designing bridges with the least material needed to hold weight, avoiding costly overbuilding. The razor doesn’t prove truth; it cuts away wasteful ideas until only the essential remains.

Three related subjects are problem-solving shortcuts, scientific skepticism rules, and how biases distort logic.
